en.extract:
The Indian peafowl (Pavo cristatus), also known as the common peafowl, or blue peafowl, is a species of peafowl native to the Indian subcontinent. While it originated in the Indian subcontinent, it has since been introduced to many other parts of the world. Male peafowl are referred to as peacocks, and female peafowl are referred to as peahens, although both sexes are often referred to colloquially as a "peacock".
The Indian peafowl displays a marked form of sexual dimorphism. The brightly coloured male has a blue coloured head with a fan-shaped crest and is best known for his long train. The train is made up of elongated upper-tail covert feathers with colourful eyespots. These stiff feathers are raised into a fan and quivered in a display during courtship. The peahen is predominantly brown in colour, with a white face and iridescent green lower neck, and lacks the elaborate train. There are several colour mutations of the Indian peafowl including the leucistic white peafowl.
Despite the length and size of the covert feathers, the peacock is still capable of flight. The peafowl lives mainly on the ground in open forests or on cultivable lands where it forages for berries and grains, and also preys on snakes, lizards and small rodents. It makes loud calls, which makes it easier to detect, and are often used to indicate the presence of a predator in the forest areas. It forages on the ground in small groups and usually escapes on foot through undergrowth and avoids flying, though it flies into tall trees to roost.
The function of the Indian peacock's elaborate train has been debated for more than a century. In the 19th century, Charles Darwin found it a puzzle, hard to explain through ordinary natural selection. His later explanation, sexual selection, is widely but not universally accepted. In the 20th century, Amotz Zahavi argued that the train was a handicap, and that males were honestly signalling their fitness in proportion to the splendour of their trains. Despite extensive study, opinions remain divided on the mechanisms involved.
The Indian peafowl is listed as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List. It is the national bird of India and venerated in Hindu and Greek mythology.


Taxonomy
The Indian peafowl was formally described in 1758 by the Swedish naturalist Carl Linnaeus in the tenth edition of his Systema Naturae under its current binomial name Pavo cristatus. The genus name Pavo is Latin for "peacock", which came from the Greek word taos derived from Persian tavus, which came from the Tamil word tokei. The specific epitet cristatus is Latin meaning "crested". The species is considered to be monotypic: no subspecies are recognised.
The earliest usage of the word peacock in written English was from the 14th century where Geoffrey Chaucer used the word in a simile "proud a pekok" in his poem Troilus and Criseyde. Various spelling variants included peacock, pacok, pecok, pekok, pokok, and pocok among others.


Description

The Indian peafowl's size, color and shape of the crest make them easily identifiable within their native distribution range. It displays a marked form of sexual dimorphism. A male peafowl or peacock is a larger sized bird with an average bill to tail length of 100–120 cm (40–46 in) and as much as 200–230 cm (78–90 in) to the end of a fully grown train. It weighs 4.1–5.2 kg (9–11.5 lb) and is amongst the heaviest birds in Phasianidae. The male has a metallic blue crown with short and curled, blue-greenish head feathers. It has a fan-shaped crest with bare black shafts and tipped with bluish-green webbing. A white stripe above the eye and a crescent shaped white patch below the eye are formed by bare white skin. The lore, chin and throat are covered with greenish feathers. It has a long blue neck with scaly bronze-green feathers with black and copper markings in the back. The scapular region and wings are made of chestnut colored primary feathers with black secondaries. The tail is dark brown with glossy green chest, buff thighs, and blackish-brown abdomen and tail coverts.

The male is best known for his elongated train, which extend from the rump. The train is made up of elongated upper tail coverts, which are bronze-green train with the outermost and longer feathers ending up with an elaborate eye-spot. The eye-spots consist of a purplish-black, heart-shaped nucleus, enclosed by blue and an outer copper rim, which is surrounded by alternating green and bronze. A few of the outer feathers lack the spot and end in a crescent shaped black tip. The feathers of the train do not have colored pigments and the colorization is a result of the micro-structure of the feathers and the optical phenomena involved. The male has a spur on the leg above the hind toe. The train feathers and the tarsal spur of the male start developing only in the second year of its life. The trains are not fully developed until the age of four. The train feathers of the male Indian peafowl are also moulted every year, usually starting at the end of the monsoon in August or September and are fully developed by February to March. The moult of the flight feathers may be spread out across the year.
The females or peahens, are smaller at around 38 cm (15 in) in length and weigh 2.7–4.1 kg (6–9 lb). The peahen has a rufous-brown head with a crest, whose tips are chestnut colored and edged with green. The upper body is brownish with pale mottling and the primaries, secondaries and tail are dark brown. The lower neck is metallic green with dark brown breast feathers glossed with green and whitish underparts. Both sexes have dark brown eyes, brown colored beak and legs. Young males also resemble the females with chestnut colored primaries.


Mutations and hybrids

There are several colour mutations of the Indian peafowl that have become common in captive birds through selective breeding. The black-shouldered mutation was initially considered as a subspecies or even a separate species of the Indian peafowl (P. nigripennis). Charles Darwin presented firm evidence for it being a variety under domestication, which is now well established and accepted. It was important for Darwin to prove that it was a colour variation rather than a wild species as it was contrary to his theory of slow modification by natural selection in the wild. In this genetic variation, the adult male is melanistic with black wings. The young birds are creamy white with fulvous-tipped wings. The gene which produces melanism in the male, causes a dilution of colour in females, which have creamy white and brown markings. Other forms of mutations include the pied and white mutations, which are the result of allelic variation at specific loci.
Crosses between a male green peafowl (Pavo muticus) and a female Indian peafowl (P. cristatus) produce a stable hybrid called a "Spalding", named after Keith Spalding, a bird fancier from California. There can be outbreeding depression if birds of unknown pedigree are released into the wild, as the viability of such hybrids and their offspring is often reduced as per Haldane's rule.


Distribution and habitat

The Indian peafowl is a resident breeder in the Indian subcontinent and is found across most of India and Sri Lanka. In India, it is found across the country from the Indus valley in the north-west to Assam in the north-east, and from Himalayas in the north to the southern tip, except for the marshlands of Sunderbans in East India. In India, it is found up to elevations of 5,000 ft (1,500 m) in the north and upto 6,000 ft (1,800 m) in the mountains of the south. In Sri Lanka, it largely inhabits the drier lowland areas. It is generally found in forests, small hills, and bushy areas near water sources. It also occupies cultivable lands and live in a semi-domesticated state in human habitations. The peafowl has since been introduced in many other parts of the world and has become feral in some areas. It was supposedly introduced into Europe by Alexander the Great, while the bird might have been introduced earlier and had reached Athens by 450 BCE.
The first whole-genome sequencing of the Indian peafowl identified 15,970 protein-coding sequences and was found to have less repetitive DNA (8.62%) than that of the chicken genome (9.45%). Studies have suggested that the population suffered at least two bottlenecks (four mya and 450,000 years ago), which resulted in a severe reduction in its effective population size.


Behaviour and ecology

The Indian peafowl forages on the ground in small groups, known as musters, that usually have a single peacock and three to five peahens. After the breeding season, the flocks tend to be made up only of females and young. It is found in the open early in the mornings and tends to rest under cover during the heat of the day. It is often sighted dust-bathing at dusk. It roosts on tall trees at night, but may sometimes make use of rocks or buildings. Birds usually arrive at their roosting sites during the dusk and call frequently before taking their positions. Despite its size, the Indian peafowl is capable of flight. However, it rarely flies and often escapes by running on foot through the undergrowth when perturbed or disturbed. When it takes off, it uses regular, slower flaps to maintain flight rather than gliding.


Diet
The Indian peafowl is found lazily feeding in the open early in the mornings, and might move to cultivated lands to feed during the mornings and the evenings. The groups often walk in single file to the nearby waterholes for a drink. The birds often congregate near human habitats and might be fed by humans. It is omnivorous and feeds on grass, seeds, flowerbuds, fruits, insects, and small reptiles. Around human settlements, it feeds on food scraps and even human excreta. Crops in its diet include peanut, tomato, paddy, chili pepper and bananas. In the Gir forest, its diet contains a large proportion of Zizyphus berries. Its diet in the Anaikatty Hills consists largely of vegetable matter and less than 9% of ants, termites, grasshoppers, beetles and earthworms.
Two Indian peafowl were observed pecking on and teasing a cobra, but they did not kill it.


Communication

The most common calls are a loud pia-ow or may-awe with the frequency of calling increasing before the monsoon season. It raises loud sounds when alarmed or disturbed and are often used to indicate the presence of a predators such as the tiger in the forests. It also makes other calls such as a rapid series of ka-aan..ka-aan or a rapid kok-kok. It often emits an explosive low-pitched honk! when agitated. It might even call at night when alarmed and neighboring birds may call in a relay like series. Nearly seven different call variants have been identified apart from six variants of alarm calls that are commonly produced by both the sexes.


Breeding

The Indian peacock is polygamous. The breeding season is associated with the onset of the monsoon rains.
Several often closely related cocks congregate at a lek site. They maintain small territories next to each other and allow females to visit them, but make no attempt to guard harems, and females do not favour specific males.
The peacock uses its ornate train in a courtship display, wherein it raises the tail feathers into an arched fan and quivers them. The wings are held half open and drooped and it periodically vibrates the long feathers, producing a ruffling sound. It faces the peahen initially and might turn around to display the tail. Display sites are usually open and elevated like fallen trees, large rocks and even roof tops.
The peacock also freezes over food to invite a peahen in a form of courtship feeding. When a peacock is displaying, peahens appear not to show any interest and usually continue foraging. Peacocks usually display in the presence of peahens, but sometimes also without them.
Cocks start shedding their train feathers between end of July in Bandipur National Park and end of October in eastern Tamil Nadu.

The Indian peafowl nests on crevices in buildings, and disused nests of vultures. Nests are usually shallow hollow scrapes on the ground lined with leaves, sticks and other debris. The female lays a clutch of three to eight oval shaped eggs. The eggs measure about 2.45–3 in (6.2–7.6 cm) in length and 1.42–2.2 in (3.6–5.6 cm) in width. They appear polished and have thick shells with pits and pores. The colour varies from pale white to buff or reddish-brown. The males take no part in hatching or rearing the young. However, isolated unusual instance of a male incubating a clutch of eggs has been reported. The eggs are incubated by the females for about 28 to 30 days.

The chicks are nidifugous and follow the mother around after hatching. Downy young may sometimes climb on their mothers' back and the female may carry them in flight to a safe tree branch.


Sexual selection

The Indian peacock is known for its brighter and elaborate colours, compared to the much duller peahen, which has been a puzzle to scientists. Charles Darwin failed to see an adaptive advantage for the extravagant tail which seemed only to be an encumbrance. He wrote to botanist Asa Gray, "the sight of a feather in a peacock's tail, whenever I gaze at it, makes me sick!". He developed the principle of sexual selection to explain the problem, however, though not everyone accepted the theory. In 1907, American artist Abbott Handerson Thayer showed in his painting that the eyespots helped form a disruptive camouflage. In his 1909 book Concealing-Coloration in the Animal Kingdom, he denied the possibility of sexual selection and argued that essentially all forms of animal coloration had evolved as camouflage. The theory was criticized by Theodore Roosevelt. 
In the 1970s, Amotz Zahavi proposed a possible resolution to the apparent contradiction between natural selection and sexual selection. He argued that the peacock honestly signalled the handicap principle of having a large and costly train. However, the mechanism may be less straightforward than it seems and the cost could be that the hormones that enhance feather development also results in the depression of the immune system. Ronald Fisher's runaway model proposed a positive feedback between female preference for elaborate trains and development of the elaborate train itself. However, this model assumes that the male train is a relatively recent evolutionary adaptation and a molecular phylogeny study shows the opposite that the most recently evolved species is actually the least ornamented one. This finding suggests a chase-away sexual selection in which females evolved resistance to the male ploy of elaborate trains".
A study on the feral population of Indian peafowl at Whipsnade Wildlife Park in southern England showed that the number of eyespots in the train predicted a male's mating success; further, this success could be manipulated by cutting the eyespots off some of the male's ornate feathers. Furthermore, the study also found that the chicks fathered by more ornamented males weighed more than those fathered by less ornamented males, which indicated an increased survival ability. However, recent studies have failed to find a relation between the number of displayed eyespots and mating success.

A seven-year study of free-ranging peafowl came to the conclusion that female peafowl do not select mates solely on the basis of their trains and it is an obsolete signal for which female preference has already been "lost or weakened". It found no evidence that peahens expressed any preference for peacocks with more elaborate trains, trains having more ocelli, a more symmetrical arrangement, or greater length. It determined that the peacock's train was not the universal target of female mate choice, and do not correlate to male physical conditions. It argued that the removal of eyespots substantially changed the appearance of male peafowls, and it was likely that the females mistook these males for sub-adults, or perceived that the males were physically damaged. Moreover, in a feral peafowl population, there is little variation in the number of eyespots in adult males as it is rare for adult males to lose a significant number of eyespots and hence, it might not form the basis for sexual selection. The British research team argued that alternative explanations for these results had been overlooked, and concluded that female choice might indeed vary in different ecological conditions.
A 2010 study on a natural population of Indian peafowls in northern India proposed a "high maintenance handicap" theory. It stated that only the fittest males can afford the time and energy to maintain a long tail and the long train is an indicator of good body condition, which results in greater mating success. While train length seems to correlate positively with major histocompatibility complex diversity in males, females do not appear to use train length to choose males. Another study in France brought up two possible explanations with the first explanation stating that there might be a genetic variation of the trait of interest under different geographical areas due to a founder effect and/or a genetic drift. The second explanation suggested that "the cost of trait expression may vary with environmental conditions," so that a trait that is indicative of a particular quality may not work in another environment. A 2013 study that tracked the eye movements of peahens responding to male displays found that the peahens looked in the direction of the upper train of feathers only when at long distances and that they looked only at the lower feathers when males displayed close to them. The rattling of the tail and the shaking of the wings helped in keeping the attention of females.


Lifespan and mortality
The Indian peafowl is known to live for up to 23 years in captivity. However, it is estimated to live for only about 15 years in the wild.
Adult Indian peafowls are difficult to capture since they can usually escape predators by flying into trees; tiger, leopard, hyena, dhole and golden jackal ambush adult individuals. Smaller peafowl are sometimes hunted by large birds of prey such as changeable hawk-eagle and rock eagle-owl. Chicks are more prone to predation than adult birds. Adults living near human habitations might sometimes be hunted by domestic dogs or by humans. The peafowls often forage in groups as it provides some safety as there are more eyes to look out for predators.


Threats and conservation

The Indian peafowl is widely distributed across India and Sri Lanka, with introduced feral colonies in many parts of the world. Conservative estimates of the population in 2002 put them at more than 100,000. While the exact population size is unknown, it is not thought to declined, and it is classified as least concern on the IUCN Red List.
Poaching of peacocks for their meat and feathers and accidental poisoning by feeding on pesticide treated seeds are known threats to wild birds. It is also hunted in some areas for usage in folk remedies. The Indian peafowl has been part of the agricultural ecosystem for centuries, where it aids in seed dispersal and feeds on pests such as insects and small rodents. However, it can cause a 40% decrease in yield in rice fields due to the damage caused. In urban areas, they can damage plants in gardens, attack their reflections thereby breaking glass and mirrors, perch and scratch cars or leave their droppings.
The Indian peafowl is protected both culturally and by law in India and Sri Lanka. Methods to identify if feathers have been plucked or have been shed naturally have been developed, as Indian law allows only the collection of feathers that have been shed. Cities have been introduced peafowl management programmes, which include education on how to prevent the birds from causing damage while treating the birds humanely. Various wildlife sanctuaries have been established in India such as Adichunchanagiri Hills, Choolannur Pea Fowl Sanctuary, and Viralimalai Bird Sanctuary for the protection of the Indian peafowl.
The Indian peafowl also breeds readily in captivity and various zoos, parks, and bird-fanciers maintain breeding populations across the world.


In culture

The Indian peafowl has been used in numerous mythological and contemporary representations. It is designated as the national bird of India. In the Indian subcontinent, many Hindu deities are associated with the bird, and it is frequently depicted in various temple art, mythology, poetry, folk music, and traditions. The Hindu war god Kartikeya is depicted as using an Indian peacock named Paravani as his vahana. Hindu god Krishna is often depicted with an Indian peacock feather on his crown. The Ramayana describes that the head of the devas, Indra, who unable to defeat the asura king Ravana, sheltered under the wing of a peacock and later blessed it with a "thousand eyes" and fearlessness from serpents. Another mythological story narrates that Indra was cursed with a thousand ulcers and was transformed into a peacock with a thousand eyes before his curse was removed by Vishnu. In Buddhist philosophy, the Indian peacock represents wisdom. 
Numerous uses of the bird's parts as an antidote to snake venom in Ayurveda and other folk remedies have been documented. It is part of folklore with beliefs such as the impregnation of peahens orally by means of the tears of the peacocks. In Greek mythology, the origin of the Indian peacock's plumage is explained in the tale of Hera and Argus. The main figure of Yezidis, Melek Taus, is commonly depicted as a peacock. A golden peacock is considered as a symbol of Ashkenazi Jewish culture, and is the subject of several folktales and songs in Yiddish. In the Middle Ages, knights in Europe took a "vow of the peacock" and decorated their helmets with its plumes. In Robin Hood stories, the titular archer uses arrows fletched with peacock feathers. Peacock feathers were buried with Viking warriors.
The birds were often kept in menageries and as ornaments in large gardens and estates in the middle ages. In 1526, the legal issue as to whether peacocks were wild or domestic fowl was thought sufficiently important for Cardinal Wolsey to summon all the English judges to give their opinion, which was that they are domestic fowl. Indian peacocks were frequently used in European heraldry with the peacocks most often depicted as facing the viewer and with the tails displayed. In this pose, the peacock is referred to as being "in his pride". Peacock tails, in isolation from the rest of the bird, are rare in British heraldry, but are used frequently in German systems. The Indian peacock feathers are used in many rituals and ornamentation and its motifs are widespread in architecture, coinage, textiles and modern items of art and utility. Indian peacock motifs are widely used even today such as in the logos of the NBC television network and SriLankan Airlines.
The term "peacocking" is often used as a means of depicting pride in English language and is used to describe someone who is very proud or gives a lot of attention to clothing. In Australia, it referred to the practice of buying up the best pieces of land so as to render the surrounding lands valueless.

The earliest usage of the word peacock in written English was from the 14th century where [[Geoffrey Chaucer]] used the word in a [[simile]] "''proud a pekok''" in his poem ''[[Troilus and Criseyde]]''.<ref>{{cite web |last=Chaucer |first=Geoffrey |title=Troilus and Criseyde |url=http://academic.brooklyn.cuny.edu/webcore/murphy/troilus/troilus1.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240604042416/http://academic.brooklyn.cuny.edu/webcore/murphy/troilus/troilus1.pdf |archive-date=4 June 2024 |access-date=1 June 2024 |work=[[University of New York]]}}</ref><ref name="Weekly">{{cite book|url=https://archive.org/stream/etymologicaldict00weekuoft#page/528/mode/1up/ |author=Weekley, E. |title=An etymological dictionary of modern English |publisher=[[John Murray (publishing house)|John Murray]] |year=1921 |isbn=978-1-176-40695-7|page=528}}</ref> Various spelling variants included peacock, pacok, pecok, pekok, pokok, and pocok among others.<ref name=Hunter/>

== Description ==
[[File:Pavo Real Venezolano.jpg|thumb|Head of a male Indian peafowl, showing his fan shaped [[Crest (feathers)|crest]]]]
The Indian peafowl's size, color and shape of the crest make them easily identifiable within their native distribution range.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://indiabiodiversity.org/species/show/238924|title=''Pavo cristatus'' |work=India biodiversity portal|access-date=1 June 2024 |archive-date=18 April 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240418023743/https://indiabiodiversity.org/species/show/238924 |url-status=live}}</ref> It displays a marked form of [[sexual dimorphism]].<ref>{{cite book |editor=Jamieson, B.G.M. |title=Reproductive Biology and Phylogeny of Birds, Part B: Sexual Selection, Behavior, Conservation, Embryology and Genetics|year=2007|isbn=978-1-482-28051-7 |publisher=Science Publishers |place=Enfield |page=377 |chapter=Applications of reproductive biology to bird conservation and population management |author1=Elphick, C.S. |author2=Reed, M. |author3=Delehanty, D.J.}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|title=Strickberger's Evolution |author1=Hall, B. K. |author2=Hallgrímsson, B. |author3=Strickberger, M. W. |year=2014|isbn=978-1-449-61484-3 |publisher=[[Jones & Bartlett]] |page=278}}</ref> A male peafowl or peacock is a larger sized bird with an average bill to tail length of {{cvt|40-46|in|cm|order=flip}} and as much as {{cvt|78-90|in|cm|order=flip}} to the end of a fully grown [[Peacock trains|train]]. It weighs {{cvt|9-11.5|lb|order=flip}} and is amongst the heaviest birds in [[Phasianidae]]. The male has a metallic blue crown with short and curled, blue-greenish head feathers. It has a fan-shaped [[Crest (feathers)|crest]] with bare black shafts and tipped with bluish-green webbing. A white stripe above the eye and a crescent shaped white patch below the eye are formed by bare white skin. The [[Lore (anatomy)|lore]], chin and throat are covered with greenish feathers. It has a long blue neck with scaly bronze-green feathers with black and copper markings in the back. The scapular region and wings are made of chestnut colored primary feathers with black secondaries. The tail is dark brown with glossy green chest, buff thighs, and blackish-brown abdomen and tail [[covert (feather)|coverts]].<ref name="Blanford">{{cite book |author1=Blanford, W. T. |author-link1=William Thomas Blanford|author2=Oates, E. W. |author-link2=Eugene William Oates |title=The Fauna of British India, Including Ceylon and Burma. Birds |pages=68–70 |volume=4 |year=1889 |publisher=[[Taylor & Francis]] |url=https://archive.org/stream/birdsindia04oaterich#page/68/mode/1up}}</ref><ref name="Whistler">{{cite book |author=Whistler, H. |author-link=Hugh Whistler |year=1949|title=Popular handbook of Indian birds |pages=407–410 |edition=4th |publisher=Gurney and Jackson |isbn=978-1-135-41787-1 |url=https://archive.org/details/popularhandbooko033226mbp/page/410/mode/1up/}}</ref>

[[File:Trevarno, pavo cristatus06.jpg|thumb|left|Close-up of the tail covert, showing the distinct eye-spot]]
The male is best known for his elongated train, which extend from the [[Rump (animal)|rump]]. The train is made up of elongated upper tail coverts, which are bronze-green train with the outermost and longer feathers ending up with an elaborate eye-spot. The eye-spots consist of a purplish-black, heart-shaped nucleus, enclosed by blue and an outer copper rim, which is surrounded by alternating green and bronze. A few of the outer feathers lack the spot and end in a crescent shaped black tip.<ref name="Blanford"/><ref name="Whistler"/> The feathers of the train do not have colored pigments and the colorization is a result of the micro-structure of the feathers and the optical phenomena involved.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Blau |first=S.K.|title=Light as a Feather: Structural Elements Give Peacock Plumes Their Color |journal=Physics Today |volume=57|issue=1|pages=18–20 |year=2004 |doi=10.1063/1.1650059 |bibcode=2004PhT....57a..18B |doi-access=free}}</ref> The male has a spur on the leg above the hind toe. The train feathers and the tarsal spur of the male start developing only in the second year of its life. The trains are not fully developed until the age of four.<ref name="Moult">{{cite journal |doi=10.2307/1366352 |url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/condor/v076n03/p0344-p0346.pdf|title=Ecological Studies of the Plumes of the Peacock (''Pavo cristatus'') |author=Sharma, I. K. |journal=The Condor |volume=76 |issue=3 |year=1974 |pages=344–346 |jstor=1366352 |access-date=24 March 2013|archive-date=20 March 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140320085057/http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/condor/v076n03/p0344-p0346.pdf|url-status=live}}</ref> The train feathers of the male Indian peafowl are also [[moulted]] every year, usually starting at the end of the [[monsoon]] in August or September and are fully developed by February to March.<ref name="Blanford"/><ref name="Moult"/> The moult of the flight feathers may be spread out across the year.<ref>{{cite journal |title=Notes on some pheasants from southwestern Asia, with remarks on molt |journal=[[American Museum Novitates]] |issue=1518 |author=Marien, D. |year=1951|pages=1–25 |hdl=2246/3909}}</ref>

The females or peahens, are smaller at around {{cvt|38|cm}} in length and weigh {{cvt|6-9|lb|order=flip}}. The peahen has a rufous-brown head with a crest, whose tips are chestnut colored and edged with green. The upper body is brownish with pale mottling and the primaries, secondaries and tail are dark brown. The lower neck is metallic green with dark brown breast feathers glossed with green and whitish underparts. Both sexes have dark brown eyes, brown colored [[beak]] and legs. Young males also resemble the females with chestnut colored primaries.<ref name="Blanford"/><ref name="Whistler"/>

=== Mutations and hybrids ===
{{multiple image |perrow=2 |image1=Black-shouldered Indian Peafowl-Naturalis Biodiversity Center.jpg |image2=Proud white peacock.JPG
|caption1=A [[melanistic]] black-shouldered Indian peafowl from [[Naturalis Biodiversity Center]], [[Leiden]]
|caption2=A displaying [[leucistic]] white Indian peafowl}}
There are several colour mutations of the Indian peafowl that have become common in captive birds through [[selective breeding]]. The black-shouldered mutation was initially considered as a subspecies or even a separate species of the Indian peafowl (''P. nigripennis'').<ref>{{cite journal |author=Sclater, P. L. |author-link=P. L. Sclater|year=1860|title=On the black-shouldered peafowl of Latham (''Pavo nigripennis'') |journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London |pages=221–222 |url=https://archive.org/stream/lietuvostsrmoksl60liet#page/221/mode/1up}}</ref> [[Charles Darwin]] presented firm evidence for it being a variety under domestication, which is now well established and accepted. It was important for Darwin to prove that it was a colour variation rather than a wild species as it was contrary to his theory of slow modification by natural selection in the wild.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Van Grouw, H. |author2=Dekkers, W. |year=2023 |title=The taxonomic history of Black-shouldered Peafowl; with Darwin's help downgraded from species to variation |journal=Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club |volume=143 |issue=1 |pages=111–121 |doi=10.25226/bboc.v143i1.2023.a7 |bibcode=2023BBrOC.143..111V |hdl=10141/623047 |hdl-access=free}}</ref> In this genetic variation, the adult male is [[melanistic]] with black wings. The young birds are creamy white with fulvous-tipped wings. The gene which produces melanism in the male, causes a dilution of colour in females, which have creamy white and brown markings.<ref name="Blanford"/><ref>{{cite magazine |title=Peafowl |author=Seth-Smith, D. |journal=Avicultural Magazine |year=1940 |volume=5 |pages=205–206}}</ref> Other forms of mutations include the pied and white mutations, which are the result of [[allele|allelic]] variation at specific [[locus (genetics)|loci]].<ref>{{cite journal |title= Plumage Color Inheritance of the Indian Blue Peafowl (''Pavo Cristatus''): Blue, Black-Shouldered, Cameo, and Oaten |journal=Journal of Heredity |year=1991 |doi=10.1093/jhered/82.1.64 |volume=82 |pages=64–68 |author1=Somes, R. G. |author2=Burger, R. E.}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=Inheritance of the White and Pied Plumage Color Patterns in the Indian Peafowl (''Pavo cristatus'') |journal=Journal of Heredity |volume=84 |issue=1 |pages=57–62 |year=1993 |author1=Somes, R. G. |author2=Burger, R. E. |doi=10.1093/oxfordjournals.jhered.a111277}}</ref>

Crosses between a male [[green peafowl]] (''Pavo muticus'') and a female Indian peafowl (''P. cristatus'') produce a stable hybrid called a "Spalding", named after Keith Spalding, a bird fancier from [[California]].<ref name="Jackson">{{cite book |author=Jackson, C. E. |title=Peacock |url=https://archive.org/details/peacockreaktionb00jack/page/n11 |url-access=limited |publisher=[[Reaktion Books]] |year=2006 |pages=10–11 |isbn=978-1-861-89293-5}}</ref> There can be [[outbreeding depression]] if birds of unknown pedigree are released into the wild, as the viability of such hybrids and their offspring is often reduced as per [[Haldane's rule]].<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1007/BF02983075|last1=Haldane|first1=J. B. S.|author-link1=J. B. S. Haldane|year=1922|title=Sex ratio and unisexual sterility in hybrid animals |url=https://zenodo.org/record/1428440|journal=[[Journal of Genetics]] |volume=12|pages=101–109 |issue=2 |s2cid=32459333 |access-date=11 September 2019|archive-date=28 November 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191128094650/https://zenodo.org/record/1428440|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Leimu |first1=R. |last2=Fischer |first2=M. |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0012614 |title=Between-Population Outbreeding Affects Plant Defence |journal=PLOS ONE |volume=5 |issue=9 |article-number=e12614 |year=2010 |pmid=20838662 |pmc=2935481 |bibcode=2010PLoSO...512614L |doi-access=free}}</ref>

== Distribution and habitat ==
[[File:Indian_peafowl_(Pavo_cristatus)_female_with_chick.jpg|thumb|Female Indian peafowl in [[Yala National Park]]]]

The Indian peafowl is a resident breeder in the [[Indian subcontinent]] and is found across most of [[India]] and [[Sri Lanka]]. In India, it is found across the country from the [[Indus Basin|Indus valley]] in the north-west to [[Assam]] in the north-east, and from [[Himalayas]] in the north to the southern tip, except for the marshlands of [[Sunderbans]] in [[East India]]. In India, it is found up to elevations of {{cvt|5000|ft}} in the north and upto {{cvt|6000|ft}} in the mountains of the south. In Sri Lanka, it largely inhabits the drier lowland areas. It is generally found in forests, small hills, and bushy areas near water sources. It also occupies cultivable lands and live in a semi-domesticated state in human habitations.<ref name="Blanford"/><ref name="Whistler"/><ref>{{cite journal|author=Dodsworth, P.T.L. |year=1912|title=Occurrence of the Common Peafowl ''Pavo cristatus'', Linnaeus in the neighbourhood of Simla, N.W. Himalayas|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=21|issue=3|pages=1082–1083|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30222511|access-date=21 December 2017 |archive-date=16 February 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190216035220/https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30222511 |url-status=live}}</ref> The peafowl has since been introduced in many other parts of the world and has become [[feral]] in some areas.<ref name="Ali"/><ref>{{cite book |author=Long, J .L. |year=1981|title=Introduced Birds of the World|publisher=Agricultural Protection Board of Western Australia}}</ref> It was supposedly introduced into [[Europe]] by [[Alexander the Great]], while the bird might have been introduced earlier and had reached [[Athens]] by 450 [[BCE]].<ref>{{cite journal |page=40| title=The birds of Old English literature |journal=The Journal of Germanic Philology |volume=2 |issue=2|year=1898 |author=Whitman, C.H. |doi=10.5962/bhl.title.54912 |url=https://archive.org/stream/cu31924031439544#page/n43/mode/1up/}}</ref><ref name="Nair">{{cite journal |year=1974 |doi=10.2307/1177550 |title=The Peacock Cult in Asia |author=Nair, P. T. |journal=Asian Folklore Studies |volume=33 |issue=2 |pages=93–170 |jstor=1177550 |url=http://www.nanzan-u.ac.jp/SHUBUNKEN/publications/afs/pdf/a272.pdf|archive-date=5 February 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090205195010/http://www.nanzan-u.ac.jp/SHUBUNKEN/publications/afs/pdf/a272.pdf}}</ref>

The first whole-genome sequencing of the Indian peafowl identified 15,970 [[Coding region|protein-coding sequences]] and was found to have less repetitive [[DNA]] (8.62%) than that of the chicken genome (9.45%). Studies have suggested that the population suffered at least two bottlenecks (four [[Million years ago|mya]] and 450,000 years ago), which resulted in a severe reduction in its effective population size.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Jaiswal| first=S.K. |author2=Gupta, A. |author3=Saxena, R. |author4=Prasoodanan, V.P. |author5=Sharma, A.K. |author6=Mittal, P. |author7=Roy, A. |author8=Shafer, A.B. |author9=Vijay, N. |author10=Sharma, V.K. |title=Genome sequence of Indian Peacock reveals the peculiar case of a glittering bird |journal=Frontiers in Genetics |year=2018 |volume=9 |page=392 |doi=10.3389/fgene.2018.00392 |doi-access=free| pmid=30283495 |pmc=6156156 }}</ref>

== Behaviour and ecology ==
[[File:Peacock Flying.jpg|thumb|An Indian peacock in flight]]

The Indian peafowl forages on the ground in small groups, known as musters, that usually have a single peacock and three to five peahens. After the breeding season, the flocks tend to be made up only of females and young. It is found in the open early in the mornings and tends to rest under cover during the heat of the day. It is often sighted dust-bathing at dusk.<ref name="Ali">{{cite book|author1=Ali, S. |author2=Ripley, S. D. |author-link1=Salim Ali|author-link2=Sidney Dillon Ripley|title=Handbook of the birds of India and Pakistan |edition=2nd |publisher=[[Oxford University Press]]|volume=2|year=1980|pages=123–126|isbn=978-0-195-62063-4}}</ref> It roosts on tall trees at night, but may sometimes make use of rocks or buildings.<ref name="Whistler"/><ref>{{cite journal|author1=Trivedi, P. |author2=Johnsingh, A.J.T. |year=1996|title=Roost selection by Indian Peafowl (''Pavo cristatus'') in Gir Forest, India|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=93|issue=1|pages=25–29 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48603263 |access-date=21 December 2017|archive-date=2 August 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200802042819/https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48603263 |url-status=live}}</ref><ref name="Roost">{{cite journal|author1=Parasharya, B. M. |author2=Mukherjee, A. |year=1999|title=Roosting behaviour of Indian Peafowl ''Pavo cristatus'' |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=96 |issue=3 |pages=471–472 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48583158 |access-date=21 December 2017 |archive-date=2 August 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200802042754/https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48583158|url-status=live}}</ref> Birds usually arrive at their roosting sites during the dusk and call frequently before taking their positions.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Navaneethakannan, K. |year=1984|title=Activity patterns in a colony of Peafowls (''Pavo cristatus'') in nature|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=81 |issue=2 |pages=387–393|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48874315|access-date=21 December 2017|archive-date=2 August 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200802042806/https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48874315|url-status=live}}</ref> Despite its size, the Indian peafowl is capable of flight. However, it rarely flies and often escapes by running on foot through the undergrowth when perturbed or disturbed. When it takes off, it uses regular, slower flaps to maintain flight rather than gliding.<ref name="Whistler"/><ref name="Ali"/>

=== Diet===
The Indian peafowl is found lazily feeding in the open early in the mornings, and might move to cultivated lands to feed during the mornings and the evenings.<ref name=Whistler/> The groups often walk in single file to the nearby waterholes for a drink.<ref name=Ali/> The birds often congregate near human habitats and might be fed by humans.<ref name=Whistler/> It is omnivorous and feeds on grass, seeds, flowerbuds, fruits, insects, and small reptiles.<ref name=Blanford/> Around human settlements, it feeds on food scraps and even human [[excreta]].<ref name=Ali/> Crops in its diet include [[peanut]], [[tomato]], [[paddy (unmilled rice)|paddy]], [[chili pepper]] and [[banana]]s.<ref name=Injar>{{cite journal |author1=Johnsingh, A.J.T. |author2=Murali, S. |year=1978 |title=The ecology and behaviour of the Indian Peafowl |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=75 |issue=4 |pages=1069–1079 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48297296 |access-date=21 December 2017 |archive-date=2 August 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200802042827/https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48297296 |url-status=live |name-list-style=amp}}</ref> In the [[Gir forest]], its diet contains a large proportion of ''[[Zizyphus]]'' berries.<ref>{{cite journal |year=1995 |title=Diet of Indian Peafowl ''Pavo cristatus'' Linn. in Gir Forest, Gujarat |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=92 |issue=2 |pages=262–263 |author1=Trivedi, P. |author2=Johnsingh, A.J.T. |name-list-style=amp |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48613807 |access-date=21 December 2017 |archive-date=2 August 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200802042815/https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48613807|url-status=live}}</ref> Its diet in the Anaikatty Hills consists largely of vegetable matter and less than 9% of [[ant]]s, [[termite]]s, [[grasshopper]]s, [[beetle]]s and [[earthworm]]s.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Rajeshkumar, N. |author2=Balasubramanian, P. |year=2011 |title=Habitat use and food habits of Indian Peafowl ''Pavo cristatus'' in Anaikatty Hills, Western Ghats |journal=Indian Birds |volume=7 |issue=5 |pages=125–127 |name-list-style=amp |url=https://indianbirds.in/pdfs/IB7.5_Rajeshkumar_Balasubramanian_IndianPeafowl.pdf}}</ref>
Two Indian peafowl were observed pecking on and teasing a cobra, but they did not kill it.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Johnsingh, A.J.T. |year=1976 |title=Peacocks and cobra |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=73 |issue=1 |page=214 |url=https://archive.org/details/journalofbombay731976bomb/page/214/mode/2up}}</ref>

=== Communication ===
[[File:Indian Peafowl.ogg|thumb|Call of Indian peafowl]]
The most common calls are a loud ''pia-ow'' or ''may-awe'' with the frequency of calling increasing before the [[monsoon]] season. It raises loud sounds when alarmed or disturbed and are often used to indicate the presence of a [[predator]]s such as the [[tiger]] in the forests. It also makes other calls such as a rapid series of ''ka-aan..ka-aan'' or a rapid ''kok-kok''. It often emits an explosive low-pitched ''honk!'' when agitated.<ref name="Whistler"/><ref name="Ali"/><ref name="Injar"/> It might even call at night when alarmed and neighboring birds may call in a relay like series. Nearly seven different call variants have been identified apart from six variants of alarm calls that are commonly produced by both the sexes.<ref>{{cite journal |journal=Journal of Ethology|title=Seasonal and diurnal use of eight different call types by Indian peafowl ( ''Pavo cristatus'')|volume=26 |issue=3 |pages=375–381 |year=2008 |author1=Takahashi, M. |author2=Hasegawa, T. |doi=10.1007/s10164-007-0078-4|s2cid=27794735}}</ref>

=== Breeding ===
[[File:Peacock Courtship and Mating Ritual in the Wild.webm|thumb|A cock courting hens]]
The Indian peacock is [[Animal sexual behaviour#Polygamy|polygamous]].<ref name=Blanford/> The breeding season is associated with the onset of the [[Monsoon of South Asia|monsoon]] rains.<ref name=Injar/><ref name=Gokula2015>{{cite journal |author1=Gokula, V. |name-list-style=amp |author2=Muthukrishnan, V. |year=2015 |title=Display behaviour of Indian Peafowl ''Pavo cristatus'' (Aves: Galliformes) during the mating season in Viralimalai, TamilNadu, India |journal=Taprobanica |volume=7 |issue=1 |pages=8–13 |doi=10.47605/tapro.v7i1.149 |url=https://tapro.sljol.info/articles/7502/files/submission/proof/7502-1-28050-1-10-20150119.pdf}}</ref>

Several often closely related cocks congregate at a [[Lek (mating arena)|lek]] site.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1038/43651 |title=Peacocks lek with relatives even in the absence of social and environmental cues |journal=Nature |volume=401 |issue=6749 |pages=155–157 |url=https://www.nbb.cornell.edu/neurobio/BioNB427/READINGS/Sherman1999.pdf |author1=Petrie, M. |name-list-style=amp |author2=Krupa, A. |author3=Burke, T. |year=1999 |bibcode=1999Natur.401..155P |s2cid=4394886 |access-date=2 June 2010 |archive-date=22 July 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110722175905/http://www.nbb.cornell.edu/neurobio/BioNB427/READINGS/Sherman1999.pdf |url-status=live}}</ref> They maintain small territories next to each other and allow females to visit them, but make no attempt to guard harems, and females do not favour specific males.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Rands, M. R. M. |author2=Ridley, M. W. |author3=Lelliott, A. D. |doi=10.1016/S0003-3472(84)80159-1 |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=830–835 |title=The social organization of feral peafowl |journal=Animal Behaviour |date=1984 |bibcode=1984AnBeh..32..830R |s2cid=53198443 |name-list-style=amp}}</ref>

The peacock uses its ornate train in a [[courtship display]], wherein it raises the tail feathers into an arched fan and quivers them.<ref name=Blanford/> The wings are held half open and drooped and it periodically vibrates the long feathers, producing a ruffling sound. It faces the peahen initially and might turn around to display the tail.<ref name=Ali/> Display sites are usually open and elevated like fallen trees, large rocks and even roof tops.<ref name=Gokula2015/>

The peacock also freezes over food to invite a peahen in a form of [[courtship feeding]].<ref>{{cite journal |url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v088n03/p0543-p0559.pdf |title=Courtship feeding in gallinaceous birds |author1=Stokes, A. W. |author2=Williams, H. W. |journal=The Auk |volume=88 |issue=3 |year=1971 |pages=543–559 |name-list-style=amp |access-date=24 March 2013 |archive-date=4 January 2014 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140104025337/http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v088n03/p0543-p0559.pdf |url-status=live}}</ref> When a peacock is displaying, peahens appear not to show any interest and usually continue foraging.<ref name=Injar/> Peacocks usually display in the presence of peahens, but sometimes also without them.<ref name=Gokula2015/><ref>{{cite journal |author1=Yasmin, S. |name-list-style=amp |author2=Yahya, H.S.A. |year=1996 |title=Correlates of mating success in Indian peafowl |journal=The Auk |volume=113 |issue=2 |pages=490–492 |doi=10.2307/4088915 |jstor=4088915 |url=https://www.academia.edu/43709052}}</ref>

Cocks start shedding their train feathers between end of July in [[Bandipur National Park]] and end of October in eastern Tamil Nadu.<ref name=Injar/>

[[File:Pavo cristatus MWNH 1075.JPG|thumb|upright=0.75|Indian peacock egg in the collection of [[Museum Wiesbaden]]]]
The Indian peafowl nests on crevices in buildings, and disused nests of [[vultures]]. Nests are usually shallow hollow scrapes on the ground lined with leaves, sticks and other debris.<ref name=Whistler/><ref>{{cite journal |author=Vyas, R. |year=1994 | title=Unusual breeding site of Indian Peafowl |journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] |volume=34 |issue=6 |page=139 |url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW34_6#page/n21/mode/1up}}</ref> The female lays a [[Clutch (eggs)|clutch]] of three to eight oval shaped eggs. The eggs measure about {{cvt|2.45-3|in|cm}} in length and {{cvt|1.42-2.2|in|cm}} in width. They appear polished and have thick shells with pits and pores. The colour varies from pale white to buff or reddish-brown. The males take no part in hatching or rearing the young.<ref name=Whistler/> However, isolated unusual instance of a male incubating a clutch of eggs has been reported.<ref name=Ali/><ref>{{cite journal |author=Shivrajkumar, Y. S. |year=1957 |title=An incubating Peacock (''Pavo cristatus'' Linn.) |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=54 |issue=2 |page=464 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48183148 |access-date=21 December 2017 |archive-date=2 August 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200802042825/https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48183148 |url-status=live}}</ref> The eggs are incubated by the females for about 28 to 30 days.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://fdc.telangana.gov.in/public/tsfdc/images/peacock_lifecycle.pdf |title=Peacock lifecycle |work=[[Government of Telangana]] |access-date=1 June 2025|archive-date=24 April 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250424055240/https://fdc.telangana.gov.in/public/tsfdc/images/peacock_lifecycle.pdf |url-status=live}}</ref>

[[File:Indian Peahens I IMG 9647.jpg|thumb|A peahen with chicks]]
The chicks are [[nidifugous]] and follow the mother around after hatching. Downy young may sometimes climb on their mothers' back and the female may carry them in flight to a safe tree branch.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Singh, H. |year=1964 |title=Peahens flying up with young |journal=Newsletter for Birdwatchers |volume=4 |issue=1 |page=14 |url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW4#page/n14/mode/1up}}</ref>

=== Sexual selection ===
[[File:PeacockInTheWoods.jpg|thumb|[[Abbott Handerson Thayer|Abott Thayer]]'s painting (1907)]]
The Indian peacock is known for its brighter and elaborate colours, compared to the much duller peahen, which has been a puzzle to scientists. [[Charles Darwin]] failed to see an adaptive advantage for the extravagant tail which seemed only to be an encumbrance. He wrote to botanist [[Asa Gray]], "the sight of a feather in a peacock's tail, whenever I gaze at it, makes me sick!". He developed the principle of [[sexual selection]] to explain the problem, however, though not everyone accepted the theory.<ref>{{cite book |last=Miller |first=G. |author-link=Geoffrey Miller (psychologist) |title=The mating mind: how sexual choice shaped the evolution of human nature |year=2000 |publisher=[[Anchor Books]] |location=[[New York City]] |isbn=978-0-385-49517-2|edition=1st}}</ref> In 1907, American artist [[Abbott Handerson Thayer]] showed in his painting that the eyespots helped form a [[disruptive camouflage]].<ref>{{cite journal |author=Fuertes Boynton |first=M. |year=1952 |title=Abbott Thayer and Natural History |journal=Osiris |volume=10 |issue=1 |pages=542–555 |doi=10.1086/368563 |s2cid=145455502}}</ref> In his 1909 book ''[[Concealing-Coloration in the Animal Kingdom]]'', he denied the possibility of sexual selection and argued that essentially all forms of [[animal coloration]] had evolved as [[camouflage]].<ref>{{cite book |author=Ball, P. |title=Invisible: The Dangerous Allure of the Unseen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=64ogBAAAQBAJ&pg=PA239 |date=2014 |publisher=[[Random House]] |isbn=978-1-84792-289-2 |page=239}}</ref> The theory was criticized by [[Theodore Roosevelt]].<ref>{{cite journal |title=Revealing and concealing coloration in birds and mammals |author=Roosevelt, T. |author-link=Theodore Roosevelt |journal=Bulletin of the American Museum of Natural History |year=1911 |volume=30 |issue=Article 8|pages=119–231 |hdl=2246/470}}</ref> 

In the 1970s, [[Amotz Zahavi]] proposed a possible resolution to the apparent contradiction between natural selection and sexual selection. He argued that the peacock [[honest signalling|honestly signalled]] the [[handicap principle]] of having a large and costly train. However, the mechanism may be less straightforward than it seems and the cost could be that the [[hormone]]s that enhance feather development also results in the depression of the [[immune system]].<ref>{{cite book |title=The handicap principle: a missing piece of Darwin's puzzle |publisher=[[Oxford University Press]] |year=1999 |isbn=0-19-512914-8 |author1=Zahavi, A. |author-link1=Amotz Zahavi|author2=Zahavi, A. |author3=Balaban, A. |author4=Ely, M. P.}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author1=Ros, A. |author2=Correia, M. |author3=Wingfield, J. |author4=Oliveira, R. |title=Mounting an immune response correlates with decreased androgen levels in male peafowl, ''Pavo cristatus'' |journal=Journal of Ethology |year=2009 |pages=209–214 |volume=27 |issue=2 |doi=10.1007/s10164-008-0105-0 |hdl=10400.12/1242 |s2cid=25651539 |hdl-access=free}}</ref> [[Ronald Fisher]]'s [[Fisherian runaway|runaway model]] proposed a positive feedback between female preference for elaborate trains and development of the elaborate train itself. However, this model assumes that the male train is a relatively recent evolutionary adaptation and a molecular phylogeny study shows the opposite that the most recently evolved species is actually the least ornamented one.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Kimball, T. T. |author2=Braun, E. L. |author3=Ligon, J. D. |author4=Lucchini, V. |author5=Randi, E. |title=A molecular phylogeny of the peacock-pheasants (Galliformes: Polyplectron spp.) indicates loss and reduction of ornamental traits and display behaviours |journal=Biological Journal of the Linnean Society |year=2001 |volume=73 |pages=187–198 |doi=10.1006/bijl.2001.0536 |issue=2 |bibcode=2001BJLS...73..187K}}</ref> This finding suggests a chase-away sexual selection in which females evolved resistance to the male ploy of elaborate trains".<ref>{{cite book|author1=Davies, N. B. |title=Introduction to Behavioural Ecology |year=2012 |publisher=[[John Wiley & Sons]] |pages=179–222 |edition=Fourth|author2=Krebs, J. R. |author3=West, S. A.}}</ref>

A study on the feral population of Indian peafowl at [[Whipsnade Wildlife Park]] in southern [[England]] showed that the number of eyespots in the train predicted a male's mating success; further, this success could be manipulated by cutting the eyespots off some of the male's ornate feathers. Furthermore, the study also found that the chicks fathered by more ornamented males weighed more than those fathered by less ornamented males, which indicated an increased survival ability.<ref name=Loyau>{{cite journal|author1=Loyau, A. |author2=Petrie, M. |author3=Jalme, M. S. |author4=Sorci, G. |title=Do peahens not prefer peacocks with more elaborate trains?|journal=Animal Behaviour |year=2008 |volume=76 |pages=e5–e9 |doi=10.1016/j.anbehav.2008.07.021 |issue=5 |bibcode=2008AnBeh..76...e5L |s2cid=40638610}}</ref> However, recent studies have failed to find a relation between the number of displayed eyespots and mating success.<ref>{{cite journal |journal=Animal Behaviour |volume=82 |issue=1 |year=2011 |pages=21–28 |title=Peahens prefer peacocks displaying more eyespots, but rarely |author1=Dakin, R. |author2=Montgomerie, R. |doi=10.1016/j.anbehav.2011.03.016|bibcode=2011AnBeh..82...21D |s2cid=53201463}}</ref>

[[File:Blue Peacock Safari Park.jpg|thumb|Close up of the open train of an Indian peacock, showing his eye-spots]]
A seven-year study of free-ranging peafowl came to the conclusion that female peafowl do not select mates solely on the basis of their trains and it is an obsolete signal for which female preference has already been "lost or weakened". It found no evidence that peahens expressed any preference for peacocks with more elaborate trains, trains having more [[Eyespot (mimicry)|ocelli]], a more symmetrical arrangement, or greater length. It determined that the peacock's train was not the universal target of female [[mate choice]], and do not correlate to male physical conditions. It argued that the removal of eyespots substantially changed the appearance of male peafowls, and it was likely that the females mistook these males for sub-adults, or perceived that the males were physically damaged. Moreover, in a feral peafowl population, there is little variation in the number of eyespots in adult males as it is rare for adult males to lose a significant number of eyespots and hence, it might not form the basis for sexual selection.<ref>{{cite journal |title=Peahens do not prefer peacocks with more elaborate trains|author1=Takahashi, M. |author2=Arita, H. |author3=Hiraiwa-Hasegawa, M. |author4=Hasegawa, T. |journal=Animal Behaviour |volume=75 |issue=4 |date=2008 |pages=1209–1219 |doi=10.1016/j.anbehav.2007.10.004 |bibcode=2008AnBeh..75.1209T }}</ref> The British research team argued that alternative explanations for these results had been overlooked, and concluded that female choice might indeed vary in different ecological conditions.<ref name="Loyau"/>

A 2010 study on a natural population of Indian peafowls in northern India proposed a "high maintenance handicap" theory. It stated that only the fittest males can afford the time and energy to maintain a long tail and the long train is an indicator of good body condition, which results in greater mating success.<ref>{{cite journal |journal=The Open Ornithology Journal |volume=3 |year=2010|pages=13–19 |title=Behavior of Indian Peafowl ''Pavo cristatus'' Linn. 1758 during the Mating Period in a Natural Population |author1=Harikrishnan, S. |author2=Vasudevan, K. |author3=Sivakumar, K. |doi=10.2174/1874453201003010013 |doi-access=free}}</ref> While train length seems to correlate positively with [[major histocompatibility complex]] diversity in males, females do not appear to use train length to choose males.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Hale, M. L. |author2=Verduijn, M. H. |author3=Moller, A. P. |author4=Wolff, K. |author5=Petrie, M. |title=Is the peacock's train an honest signal of genetic quality at the major histocompatibility complex? |journal=Journal of Evolutionary Biology |year=2009 |volume=22 |pages=1284–1294 |doi=10.1111/j.1420-9101.2009.01746.x |issue=6 |pmid=19453370 |doi-access=free}}</ref> Another study in France brought up two possible explanations with the first explanation stating that there might be a genetic variation of the trait of interest under different geographical areas due to a [[founder effect]] and/or a [[genetic drift]]. The second explanation suggested that "the cost of trait expression may vary with environmental conditions," so that a trait that is indicative of a particular quality may not work in another environment.<ref name=Loyau/> A 2013 study that tracked the eye movements of peahens responding to male displays found that the peahens looked in the direction of the upper train of feathers only when at long distances and that they looked only at the lower feathers when males displayed close to them. The rattling of the tail and the shaking of the wings helped in keeping the attention of females.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1242/jeb.087338 |year=2013 |journal=Journal of Experimental Biology |volume=216 |issue=16 |pages=3035–3046 |title=Through their eyes: selective attention in peahens during courtship |first1=J. L. |last1=Yorzinski |first2=G. L. |last2=Patricelli |first3=J. S.|last3=Babcock |first4=J. M.|last4=Pearson|first5= M. L. |last5=Platt |pmid=23885088 |pmc=4074220 |bibcode=2013JExpB.216.3035Y}}</ref>

=== Lifespan and mortality ===
The Indian peafowl is known to live for up to 23 years in captivity. However, it is estimated to live for only about 15 years in the wild.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Flower, M. S. S. |year=1938 |title=The duration of life in animals – IV. Birds: special notes by orders and families| journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London| pages=195–235}}</ref>

Adult Indian peafowls are difficult to capture since they can usually escape [[Predation|predator]]s by flying into trees; [[tiger]], [[leopard]], [[hyena]], [[dhole]] and [[golden jackal]] ambush adult individuals.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Hayward, M. W. |author2=Jędrzejewski, W. |author3=Jedrzejewska, B. |title=Prey preferences of the tiger ''Panthera tigris'' |journal=Journal of Zoology |volume=286 |issue=3 |year=2012 |pages=221–231 |doi=10.1111/j.1469-7998.2011.00871.x}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author1=Arviazhagan, C. |author2=Arumugam, R. |author3=Thiyagesan, K. |title=Food habits of leopard (''Panthera pardus fusca''), dhole (''Cuon alpinus'') and striped hyena (''Hyaena hyaena'') in a tropical dry thorn forest of southern India |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |year=2007|volume=104|pages=178–187}}</ref> Smaller peafowl are sometimes hunted by large birds of prey such as [[changeable hawk-eagle]] and [[rock eagle-owl]].<ref>{{cite journal |author=Dhanwatey, A. S. |year=1986 |title=A Crested Hawk-Eagle ''Spizaetus cirrhatus'' (Gmelin) killing a Peafowl ''Pavo cristatus'' Linnaeus |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=83 |issue=4 |page=202 |access-date=21 December 2017 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48772392 |archive-date=2 August 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200802042809/https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48772392|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |year=1990 |title=Indian Great Horned Owl ''Bubo bubo'' (Linn.) and Peafowl ''Pavo cristatus'' Linn |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=87 |issue=2 |page=300 |author1=Tehsin, R. |author2=Tehsin, F. |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48807014 |access-date=21 December 2017|archive-date=14 October 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181014015230/https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48807014 |url-status=live}}</ref> Chicks are more prone to predation than adult birds. Adults living near human habitations might sometimes be hunted by domestic [[dog]]s or by humans.<ref name=Injar/> The peafowls often forage in groups as it provides some safety as there are more eyes to look out for predators.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Shahla, Y. |author2=Yahya, H. S. A. |year=2000 |title=Group size and vigilance in Indian Peafowl ''Pavo cristatus'' (Linn.), Family: Phasianidae |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=97|issue=3|pages=425–428 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48567869|access-date=21 December 2017|archive-date=2 August 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200802042838/https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48567869|url-status=live}}</ref>

== Threats and conservation ==
[[File:Peacock Dance.jpg|thumb|A male Indian peafowl at [[Yala National Park]] in [[Sri Lanka]]]]
The Indian peafowl is widely distributed across India and Sri Lanka, with introduced feral colonies in many parts of the world. Conservative estimates of the population in 2002 put them at more than 100,000.<ref>{{cite book|author1=Madge, S. |author2=McGowan, P. |year=2002 |title=Pheasant, partridges and grouse, including buttonquails, sandgrouse and allies |publisher=Christopher Helm |place=London}}</ref> While the exact population size is unknown, it is not thought to declined, and it is classified as [[least concern]] on the [[IUCN Red List]].<ref name=iucn/>

Poaching of peacocks for their meat and feathers and accidental poisoning by feeding on pesticide treated seeds are known threats to wild birds.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Alexander, J. P. |year=1983|title=Probable diazinon poisoning in peafowl: a clinical description |journal=   Veterinary Record|volume=113 |issue=20 |page=470|doi=10.1136/vr.113.20.470|doi-broken-date=5 April 2026 |pmid=6649386 |s2cid=11252054}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author1=Ramesh, K. |author2=McGowan, P. |year=2009|title=On the current status of Indian Peafowl ''Pavo cristatus'' (Aves: Galliformes: Phasianidae): keeping the common species common|journal=Journal of Threatened Taxa |volume=1 |issue=2 |pages=106–108|doi=10.11609/jott.o1845.106-8|doi-access=free}}</ref> It is also hunted in some areas for usage in folk remedies.<ref name=Injar/> The Indian peafowl has been part of the agricultural ecosystem for centuries, where it aids in seed dispersal and feeds on pests such as insects and small rodents. However, it can cause a 40% decrease in yield in rice fields due to the damage caused.<ref name=Ali/><ref>{{cite web |author=Ramanan, S.S. |title=Researchers quantify damage caused by peafowl to farmers |date=2018 |work=[[Down to Earth (magazine)|Down to Earth]] |url=https://www.downtoearth.org.in/wildlife-biodiversity/researchers-quantify-damage-caused-by-peafowl-to-farmers-62441 |access-date=1 June 2024}}</ref><ref name=Agro>{{cite web |title=Sri Lanka's divine pests: Peafowl problem calls for human action |author=Amarasinge, T. |date=2020 |url=https://news.mongabay.com/2020/01/sri-lankas-divine-pests-peafowl-problem-calls-for-human-action-commentary/|access-date=1 June 2024 |work=[[Mongabay]] |archive-date=31 May 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240531114945/https://news.mongabay.com/2020/01/sri-lankas-divine-pests-peafowl-problem-calls-for-human-action-commentary/|url-status=live}}</ref> In urban areas, they can damage plants in gardens, attack their reflections thereby breaking glass and mirrors, perch and scratch cars or leave their droppings.<ref>{{cite web |title=Peafowl |work=City of San Antonio |url=https://www.sa.gov/Directory/Departments/ACS/Wildlife/Peafowl|access-date=1 June 2024 |archive-date=27 June 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240627013845/https://www.sa.gov/Directory/Departments/ACS/Wildlife/Peafowl|url-status=live}}</ref>

The Indian peafowl is protected both culturally and by law in India and Sri Lanka.<ref name=Whistler/><ref name=Agro/> Methods to identify if feathers have been plucked or have been shed naturally have been developed, as Indian law allows only the collection of feathers that have been shed.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.scijus.2007.08.002|author=Sahajpal, V. |author2=Goyal, S. P. |year=2008 |title=Identification of shed or plucked origin of Indian Peafowl (''Pavo cristatus'') tail feathers: Preliminary findings |journal=Science and Justice |volume=48 |issue=2 |pages=76–78 |pmid=18700500}}</ref> Cities have been introduced peafowl management programmes, which include education on how to prevent the birds from causing damage while treating the birds humanely.<ref>{{cite web |title=La Canada, California, City Council, Peafowl Management Plan Update |url=http://www.lacanadaflintridge.com/city/council/agendaitems/2009/120709/item8.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110607171148/http://www.lacanadaflintridge.com/city/council/agendaitems/2009/120709/item8.pdf |archive-date=7 June 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.east-northamptonshire.gov.uk/downloads/Peafowl_Peacocks.pdf |title=East Northamptonshire plan |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110613203116/http://www.east-northamptonshire.gov.uk/downloads/Peafowl_Peacocks.pdf|archive-date=13 June 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.dunedingov.com/docs/leisureservices/livingwithpeafowl.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081221082738/http://www.dunedingov.com/docs/leisureservices/livingwithpeafowl.pdf |archive-date=21 December 2008 |title=Living with peafowl. City of Dunedin, Florida }}</ref> Various wildlife sanctuaries have been established in India such as [[Adichunchanagiri Hills]], [[Choolannur Pea Fowl Sanctuary]], and [[Viralimalai Bird Sanctuary]] for the protection of the Indian peafowl.<ref>{{cite report |url=https://tourism.gov.in/sites/default/files/2023-04/9%20Compendium%20Book_2021_Low%20%281%29%20%282%29.pdf |title=National Parks and Wildlife Sanctuaries in India |work=[[Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change]], [[Government of India]] |date=2021|access-date=1 June 2024}}</ref>

The Indian peafowl also breeds readily in captivity and various zoos, parks, and bird-fanciers maintain breeding populations across the world.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.sfzoo.org/indian-peafowl/|title=Indian peafowl|work=[[San Francisco Zoo]]|access-date=1 June 2024}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://animals.sandiegozoo.org/animals/peafowl|title=Indian peafowl|work=[[San Diego Zoo]]|access-date=1 June 2024 |archive-date=5 March 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210305142514/https://animals.sandiegozoo.org/animals/peafowl|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://zoobarcelona.cat/en/animals/indian-peafowl|title=Indian peafowl|work=[[Barcelona Zoo]]|access-date=1 June 2024|archive-date=22 April 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240422085315/https://zoobarcelona.cat/en/animals/indian-peafowl|url-status=live}}</ref>

==In culture==
[[File:Murugan by Raja Ravi Varma.jpg|thumb|[[Kartikeya]] with his consorts riding a peacock, painting by [[Raja Ravi Varma]]]]
The Indian peafowl has been used in numerous mythological and contemporary representations.<ref name="Ali"/> It is designated as the [[national bird]] of India.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://knowindia.india.gov.in/national-identity-elements/national-bird.php|title=National Bird|access-date=3 April 2024|publisher=[[Government of India]]|archive-date=29 October 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211029051307/https://knowindia.india.gov.in/national-identity-elements/national-bird.php|url-status=live}}</ref> In the Indian subcontinent, many [[Hindu deities]] are associated with the bird, and it is frequently depicted in various temple art, mythology, poetry, folk music, and traditions.<ref>{{cite journal |title=Folklore of birds and beasts of India|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=28|issue=2|pages=562–565|author=Fitzpatrick, J. |year=1923|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30113154|access-date=21 December 2017|archive-date=2 August 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200802042751/https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30113154|url-status=live}}</ref> The Hindu war god [[Kartikeya]] is depicted as using an Indian peacock named Paravani as his [[vahana]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://disa.ukzn.ac.za/gandhi-luthuli-documentation-centre/vehicle-lord-murugan-rides-peacock-called-paravani|title=The Vehicle Lord Murugan rides is a peacock called Paravani |publisher=Gandhi Luthuli Documentation Center |access-date=1 December 2023|archive-date=24 September 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230924172622/https://disa.ukzn.ac.za/gandhi-luthuli-documentation-centre/vehicle-lord-murugan-rides-peacock-called-paravani|url-status=live}}</ref> Hindu god [[Krishna]] is often depicted with an Indian peacock feather on his crown. The ''[[Ramayana]]'' describes that the head of the [[Deva (Hinduism)|deva]]s, [[Indra]], who unable to defeat the [[asura]] king [[Ravana]], sheltered under the wing of a peacock and later blessed it with a "thousand eyes" and fearlessness from serpents.<ref name="Lal">{{cite book|title=Peacock in Indian art, thought and literature|author=Lal, K. |publisher=Abhinav Publications|year=2007|isbn=978-8-17017-429-5|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wuotb7YyrigC |pages=11, 26, 139}}</ref> Another mythological story narrates that Indra was cursed with a thousand ulcers and was transformed into a peacock with a thousand eyes before his curse was removed by [[Vishnu]].<ref>{{cite book|url=https://archive.org/stream/ramavijayathemyt00unwkuoft#page/n27/mode/1up/|page=14 |title= Ramavijaya (The mythological history of Rama)| year=1891|publisher=Dubhashi and company}}</ref> In [[Buddhist]] philosophy, the Indian peacock represents wisdom.<ref>{{cite journal |url=http://ccbs.ntu.edu.tw/FULLTEXT/JR-BH/bh117490.htm |year=1988|title=Symbolism of Animals in Buddhism|author=Choskyi, V. J. |journal=Buddhist Hiamalaya |volume=1|issue=1 |access-date=1 June 2010|archive-date=29 May 2010|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100529055901/http://ccbs.ntu.edu.tw/FULLTEXT/JR-BH/bh117490.htm|url-status=live}}</ref> 

Numerous uses of the bird's parts as an antidote to snake venom in [[Ayurveda]] and other folk remedies have been documented.<ref>{{cite web |last=Challinor |first=D. |author-link=David Challinor |title=Letter from the Desk of David Challinor, November 2001 |url=http://si-pddr.si.edu/dspace/bitstream/10088/1193/1/November_2001.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110827064244/http://si-pddr.si.edu/dspace/bitstream/10088/1193/1/November_2001.pdf |archive-date=27 August 2011 |access-date=2 June 2010 |publisher=[[Smithsonian Institution]]}}</ref> It is part of folklore with beliefs such as the impregnation of peahens orally by means of the tears of the peacocks.<ref>{{cite book|first=E. |last=Rolland|year=1915 |title=Faune populaire de la France. Tome 6.|page=149|url=http://gallica.bnf.fr/ark:/12148/bpt6k5433286x/f161.image|access-date=2 June 2017|archive-date=11 August 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170811120632/http://gallica.bnf.fr/ark:/12148/bpt6k5433286x/f161.image|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=Emeneau |first=M. B. |author-link=Murray Barnson Emeneau |year=1943 |title=Studies in the Folk-Tales of India: I: Some Origin Stories of the Todas and Kotas |journal=Journal of the American Oriental Society |volume=63 |issue=2 |pages=158–168 |doi=10.2307/594123 |jstor=594123}}</ref> In [[Greek mythology]], the origin of the Indian peacock's plumage is explained in the tale of [[Hera]] and [[Argus Panoptes|Argus]].<ref name="Jackson"/> The main figure of [[Yezidi]]s, [[Melek Taus]], is commonly depicted as a peacock.<ref>{{cite book|title=The cult of the peacock angel |author=Empson, R. H. W. |publisher=[[H. F and G. Witherby]]|year=1928|url=https://archive.org/details/MN40203ucmf_2}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|author=Springett, B. H. |title= Secret sects of Syria and the Lebanon|publisher=[[George Allen & Unwin]]|year=1922|url=https://archive.org/details/secretsectsofsyr032392mbp}}</ref> A [[Di Goldene Pave|golden peacock]] is considered as a symbol of [[Yiddishkeit|Ashkenazi Jewish culture]], and is the subject of several folktales and songs in Yiddish.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://ingeveb.org/blog/introducing-in-geveb-a-journal-of-yiddish-studies|title=A Peacock's Dream: Introducing In geveb|work=In geveb|access-date=2019-06-04|archive-date=4 June 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190604163633/https://ingeveb.org/blog/introducing-in-geveb-a-journal-of-yiddish-studies|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.jewishfolksongs.com/en/golden-peacock|title=The Golden Peacock {{!}} Jewish Folk Songs |work=Jewish Folk Songs|access-date=4 June 2019|archive-date=29 March 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170329051221/http://www.jewishfolksongs.com/en/golden-peacock|url-status=live}}</ref> In the [[Middle Ages]], knights in Europe took a "vow of the peacock" and decorated their helmets with its plumes. In [[Robin Hood]] stories, the titular archer uses arrows fletched with peacock feathers. Peacock feathers were buried with [[Viking]] warriors.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Tyrberg, T. |year=2002|title=The archaeological record of domesticated and tamed birds in Sweden |journal=Acta Zoologica Cracoviensia |volume=45 |pages=215–231|url=http://www.isez.pan.krakow.pl/journals/azc_v/pdf/45/16.pdf|access-date=2 June 2010 |archive-date=26 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110726075816/http://www.isez.pan.krakow.pl/journals/azc_v/pdf/45/16.pdf}}</ref>

The birds were often kept in menageries and as ornaments in large gardens and estates in the middle ages. In 1526, the legal issue as to whether peacocks were wild or domestic fowl was thought sufficiently important for [[Cardinal Wolsey]] to summon all the English judges to give their opinion, which was that they are domestic fowl.<ref>{{cite book|author=Gwyn, P. |title=The King's Cardinal: The rise and Fall of Thomas Wolsey|publisher=Pimlico|year=2000|page=113|isbn=978-0-71265-122-6}}</ref> Indian peacocks were frequently used in European heraldry with the peacocks most often depicted as facing the viewer and with the tails displayed. In this pose, the peacock is referred to as being "in his pride". Peacock tails, in isolation from the rest of the bird, are rare in British heraldry, but are used frequently in [[German people|German]] systems.<ref>{{cite book|author=Fox-Davies, A. |author-link=Arthur Fox-Davies|title=A Complete Guide to Heraldry|publisher=T.C. and E.C. Jack|year=1909|pages=246–247|url=https://archive.org/details/completeguidetoh00foxduoft}}</ref> The Indian peacock feathers are used in many rituals and ornamentation and its motifs are widespread in architecture, coinage, textiles and modern items of art and utility.<ref name="Nair"/> Indian peacock motifs are widely used even today such as in the logos of the [[NBC logo|NBC]] television network and [[SriLankan Airlines]].<ref>{{cite web|title=Company Quietly Adds NBC Peacock To Corporate Logo|url=https://deadline.com/2012/12/comcast-flips-the-bird-company-quietly-adds-nbc-peacock-to-corporate-logo-386360/|author=Finke, N. |work=[[Deadline Hollywood]]|date=2012|access-date=6 February 2013|archive-date=2 April 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150402201146/http://deadline.com/2012/12/comcast-flips-the-bird-company-quietly-adds-nbc-peacock-to-corporate-logo-386360/|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.zilliondesigns.com/blog/best-international-airline-logos-with-mythological-creatures/|title=Best International Airline Logos with Mythological Creatures |date=2014|access-date=1 June 2024|work=Zillion designs}}</ref>

The term "peacocking" is often used as a means of depicting pride in [[English language]] and is used to describe someone who is very proud or gives a lot of attention to clothing.<ref name="Hunter"/><ref>{{cite web|url=http://dictionary.cambridge.org/dictionary/british/peacock|title=Peacock|website=Cambridge English Dictionary|access-date=2 June 2010 |archive-date=8 September 2010|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100908053620/http://dictionary.cambridge.org/dictionary/british/peacock|url-status=live}}</ref> In Australia, it referred to the practice of buying up the best pieces of land so as to render the surrounding lands valueless.<ref>{{cite book|title=A dictionary of slang and unconventional English |author1=Partridge, E. |author2=Beale, P. |year=2002 |publisher=[[Routledge]]|isbn=978-0-415-29189-7}}</ref>
